content.problemStatement:
Chef's phone shows a Battery Low notification if the battery level is 15%15 \%15% or less.

Given that the battery level of Chef's phone is X%X \%X%, determine whether it would show a Battery low notification.


Input Format

First line will contain TTT, number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
Each test case contains a single line of input, an integer XXX, denoting the battery level of the phone.


Output Format
For each test case, output in a single line Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes, if the battery level is 15%15 \%15% or below. Otherwise, print No\text{No}No.


You may print each character of Yes\texttt{Yes}Yes and No\text{No}No in uppercase or lowercase (for example, YeS\texttt{YeS}YeS, YES\texttt{YES}YES, yes\texttt{yes}yes will be considered identical).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the battery level (in percent).
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a line containing "Yes" if X is less than or equal to 15, otherwise output "No". The case of letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation: Battery levels 15 and 3 are ≤15 so the notification appears (Yes). Level 65 is >15 so output is No.
First test case X=15 → Yes. Second test case X=16 → No.

solution.approach: Read the number of test cases. For each test case read X and compare it with the threshold 15. If X <= 15 print "Yes", else print "No". This is a direct O(1) check per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time comparison for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used regardless of input size.

code.C:
#include <stdio.h>
int main(){int T; if(scanf("%d",&T)!=1) return 0; while(T--){int X; scanf("%d",&X); if(X<=15) printf("Yes\n"); else printf("No\n");} return 0;}
